summary: French king (r. 1643-1715) nicknamed the "Sun King" (le Roi Soleil), remembered for the (possibly apocryphal) declaration "I am the state" (L'état, c'est moi) and for the longest reign of any monarch in history. Came to power as a minor under the regency of Cardinal Mazarin, who put down the noble/parlementaire uprising known as the Fronde. Transformed the Palace of Versailles from a hunting lodge into Europe's grandest royal residence, patronizing painter Charles Le Brun, architect Jules Hardouin-Mansart, and composer Jean-Baptiste Lully. Revoked the Edict of Nantes (Edict of Fontainebleau, 1685), persecuting Huguenots, Jansenists, and Quietists while asserting Gallican independence from the papacy. Fought a chain of wars — the War of Devolution, the Franco-Dutch War, the War of the League of Augsburg, and the War of the Spanish Succession — the last installing his grandson Philip of Anjou as Philip V of Spain. His court was rocked by the Affair of the Poisons, implicating his mistress Madame de Montespan; he later secretly married Madame de Maintenon.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Louis XIV is identified in giveaways: the French king behind the Palace of Versailles, nicknamed the "Sun King" (le Roi Soleil), famed for the declaration "I am the state" (L'état, c'est moi), and holder of the longest reign of any monarch in history at 72 years.

clues.clue: Commissioned/ordered the transformation of the Palace of Versailles from a hunting lodge into a grand royal residence
examples: name this French king who commissioned the Palace of Versailles in the 1660s.
name this "Sun King," who ordered the transformation of the Palace of Versailles into a royal residence.

clues.clue: Declared (possibly apocryphally) "I am the state" (L'état, c'est moi)
examples: uttered "I am the state."
This king revoked the Edict of Nantes and stated "l'etat, c'est moi."

clues.clue: Nicknamed the "Sun King" (le Roi Soleil); depictions of his face often carry the motto "Nec pluribus impar"
examples: For 10 points, name this "Sun King" of France.
Depictions of his face are often accompanied by the motto "Nec pluribus impar."

clues.clue: Reigned 72 years, the longest reign of any monarch in history
examples: Vauban both designed and broke forts for this French king during the many wars he fought during his 72-year reign, the longest of any monarch in history.
The installation of Philip V as the Bourbon king of Spain was a success for this French "Sun King", Philip's grandfather who passed away a year later after 72 years of rule.

works.name: The Fronde and the Regency of Cardinal Mazarin
works.description: During Louis's minority, Cardinal Jules Mazarin — successor to the "Red Eminence" — governed as chief minister and tutored the young king while putting down the Fronde, a mid-17th-century civil war of noble and parlementaire revolt named for the slingshots used by rioting Parisian crowds; its suppression cemented Louis's absolutist model of kingship.

clues.clue: Cardinal Jules Mazarin, who succeeded the "Red Eminence," governed as chief minister (1642-1661) and tutored Louis during his regency, also signing the Peace of Westphalia
examples: This king was tutored by Cardinal Mazarin, who signed the Peace of Westphalia and dealt with the Fronde as chief minister during this king's regency.
Name this Cardinal who was chief minister of France from 1642 to 1661, succeeding another cardinal known as the "Red Eminence."

clues.clue: The Fronde, a civil war named for the slingshots ("frondes") used by rioting crowds, unfolded in phases led by the parlements and then the princes of the blood (including the Grand Condé's revolt); its suppression became an early model for absolutist governance
examples: Name this mid 17th century civil war which ended in the triumph of royal power in France. It is named for a weapon used by rioting Parisian crowds.
The Fronde was a revolt against this "Sun King" of France, who was an early model for absolutist governance in Europe.

clues.clue: The Battle of the Faubourg St. Antoine was fought during the Fronde
examples: The Battle of the Faubourg St Antoine was fought during a rebellion against this ruler that was started by local appellate courts called parlements and named for slingshots used to break windows.

clues.clue: The Fronde broke out in the aftermath of the Thirty Years' War (ended by the 1648 Treaty of Westphalia), after France's victory at the Battle of Rocroi crushed Spanish military dominance
examples: The Fronde occurred in the aftermath of this large European conflict, which was ended by the 1648 Treaty of Westphalia. Spanish military dominance was crushed after France's victory in this war's Battle of Rocroi.

clues.clue: As part of his centralizing administration after the Fronde, Louis relied on intendants — officials temporarily appointed from the nobles of the robe to enforce royal power in the provinces
examples: As part of Louis XIV's centralizing administration, these officials were temporarily appointed from among the nobles of the robe and used as inspectors to enforce royal power in the provinces.

works.name: Wars and Diplomacy
works.description: Louis fought a near-continuous series of wars to expand and secure France's borders: the War of Devolution over his wife's unpaid dowry, the Franco-Dutch War settled at Nijmegen, the War of the League of Augsburg (Grand Alliance) that pitted him against a coalition led by William III, and finally the War of the Spanish Succession, fought to install his grandson on the Spanish throne.

clues.clue: Signed the Treaty of the Pyrenees (1659) with Philip IV of Spain, ending a war that included the Battle of the Dunes
examples: Name these two kings of neighboring countries. They signed the Treaty of the Pyrenees to end a war between their countries that included the Battle of the Dunes.

clues.clue: The unpaid dowry from his marriage to Maria Theresa of Spain, following the death of her father Philip IV, led Louis to launch the War of Devolution
examples: The dowry for Louis' marriage to this Spanish princess was never paid, leading to the War of Devolution.
The marriage of these people to Princes of the Blood caused controversy in the court of the father of these people, who instigated the War of Devolution.

clues.clue: Fought the Franco-Dutch War (invading the United Provinces after signing the Treaty of Dover with England), ended by the Treaties of Nijmegen (1678-1679), which gave France significant holdings in modern Belgium
examples: This king signed the treaty of Dover with England, who then aided him when he invaded the United Provinces.
These treaties, signed in 1678 and 1679, ended the Franco-Dutch War, giving France significant holdings in modern Belgium.

clues.clue: Fought the War of the League of Augsburg (War of the Grand Alliance / Nine Years' War) against a coalition of the Holy Roman Empire, Spain, and Britain (led by William III) resisting his territorial expansion; his engineer Vauban both captured and later defended Namur during the war
examples: He opposed King William III of England during the War of the League of Augsburg.

Both the French capture and subsequent defense of Namur occurred during this war, which is named for the coalition of the Holy Roman Empire, Spain, Britain, and others that formed to resist Louis's territorial expansions.

clues.clue: Suggested/placed his grandson Philip of Anjou on the throne of Spain as Philip V after the death of Charles II, sparking the War of the Spanish Succession
examples: This ruler, who suggested Philip of Anjou to succeed Charles II, kicking off the War of Spanish Succession, uttered "I am the state."
Name this French king who succeeded in placing his grandson Philip of Anjou on the throne of Spain after the War of the Spanish Succession.

clues.clue: At the resulting peace negotiations at Utrecht, the House of Bourbon was forced to grant Britain the asiento (slave-trade monopoly) and was split into separate French and Spanish lines
examples: Name this dynasty which was forced to grant the asiento monopoly on the slave trade with its colonies to British merchants during peace negotiations at Utrecht, which also split this royal house into two distinct lines.

works.name: Religious Policy and Persecution
works.description: Louis pursued religious uniformity throughout his reign: he revoked toleration for Protestants, persecuted Jansenists and Quietists, and asserted French Gallican independence from papal authority, repeatedly clashing with Rome.

clues.clue: Revoked the Edict of Nantes with the 1685 Edict of Fontainebleau, ending religious toleration and driving most French Huguenots to flee
examples: This monarch revoked religious toleration with the Edict of Fontainebleau, leading most of his country's Huguenots to flee.
This absolute monarch revoked the Edict of Nantes in 1685's Edict of Fontainebleau.

clues.clue: Forced religious minorities (Huguenots) to quarter destructive, unruly soldiers under his dragonnade policy
examples: This king, who razed the Jansenist stronghold at Port-Royal Abbey, forced religious minorities to quarter destructive and unruly soldiers with his dragonnade policy.

clues.clue: Razed the Jansenist stronghold of Port-Royal Abbey
examples: This king, who razed the Jansenist stronghold at Port-Royal Abbey, forced religious minorities to quarter destructive and unruly soldiers with his dragonnade policy.

clues.clue: Requested the apostolic constitution Regiminis Apostolici from Pope Alexander VII, requiring clergy to subscribe to a "formula for submission," sparking the Formulary Controversy
examples: This monarch requested the apostolic constitution Regiminis Apostolici from Pope Alexander VII, which required all clergy to subscribe to a "formula for submission" and led to the Formulary Controversy.

clues.clue: Promulgated the 1682 Declaration of the Clergy of France (welcomed by Antoine Arnauld), codifying Gallicanism — the doctrine, championed by Pierre Pithou, that the Pope held only spiritual, not temporal, jurisdiction — causing a long feud with the papacy
examples: To the plaudits of Antoine Arnauld, this king promulgated the pro-absolutist 1682 Declaration of the Clergy of France, causing a long feud with the Pope.

The Declaration of the Clergy of France codified and promulgated this French anti-ultramontanist doctrine championed by Pierre Pithou that holds that the Pope had spiritual, but not temporal, jurisdiction.

clues.clue: At a clergy assembly, created the Gallican liberties, which Pope Innocent XI annulled after also vetoing Louis's choice for Prince-elector of Cologne; Louis feuded chiefly with Innocent XI but found some detente with Innocent XII
examples: At a clergy assembly, this ruler created a set of privileges known as the Gallican liberties which were annulled by Pope Innocent XI, who vetoed this ruler's selection for Prince-elector of Cologne.
Louis particularly feuded with the 11th pope of this name, but had some detente with the 12th.

clues.clue: The mystic Jeanne Guyon convinced Louis's secret wife (Madame de Maintenon) to adopt Quietism; Maintenon in turn used her influence to have Jacques-Bénigne Bossuet persecuted for advancing the doctrine, which was condemned by Innocent XII
examples: The mystic Jeanne Guyon briefly convinced this king's secret wife to adopt Quietism.
Francoise Scarron used her influence with the king to persecute Jacques-Benigne Bossuet for advancing this religious doctrine.

clues.clue: His nephew Victor Amadeus II of Savoy massacred the Waldensians; Louis himself revoked the Edict of Nantes from his palace at Versailles
examples: The Waldensians were massacred by the forces of Victor Amadeus II of Savoy, a nephew of this king. This king revoked the Edict of Nantes from his palace at Versailles.

clues.clue: The assassination of François Langlade was carried out by Abraham Mazel and a band of Camisards during his reign
examples: The assassination of Francois Langlade was carried out by Abraham Mazel and a group of Camisards under the rule of this ruler.

clues.clue: In 1685, issued the Code Noir (drafted by Colbert and the Marquis de Seignelay), regulating slavery in the French Empire, including a requirement that all slaves be baptized Catholic
examples: Louis issued this decree, written by Jean-Baptiste Colbert and the Marquis de Seignelay, in 1685. It introduced various regulations for slavery in the French Empire, including the requirement that all slaves be baptized Catholic.

works.name: Ministers and Servants of the Crown: Colbert, Fouquet, and Vauban
works.description: Beyond Mazarin, Louis relied on a succession of powerful servants: finance minister Jean-Baptiste Colbert, who built mercantilist gold-focused economic policy; his predecessor Nicolas Fouquet, whose extravagant self-display doomed him; and the military engineer Vauban, who designed and besieged France's fortresses across four decades of war.

clues.clue: Jean-Baptiste Colbert, an intendant turned finance minister, built economic policy around accumulating gold and maintained the Maison du Roi as Superintendent of Buildings
examples: Name this former intendant who, as finance minister, based his economic policies around the accumulation of gold. He also maintained the Maison du Roi as Superintendent of Buildings.
Colbert served under this French King, who endured the Fronde rebellion during his youth.

clues.clue: Colbert ordered construction of the Canal du Midi, connecting Toulouse to the Mediterranean
examples: Colbert ordered the construction of the Canal du Midi, which connected this French city to the Mediterranean.

clues.clue: Nicolas Fouquet, Louis's earlier finance minister, fell from favor after an extravagant party at his chateau of Vaux-le-Vicomte and died imprisoned at the fortress of Pignerol
examples: Identify this minister whose downfall came when he gave a lavish party at his opulent chateau at Vaux-le-Vicomte.
This man died serving a prison term at the fortress of Pignerol.

clues.clue: Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban, Louis's military engineer, developed ricochet-firing technique and designed/besieged star-shaped fortresses including the Citadel of Lille and (against Dutch counterpart Menno van Coehoorn) Namur, and expanded Luxembourg's Bock fortress
examples: Name this man who developed the technique of ricochet firing, in which projectiles were counterintuitively launched at lower angles and lower power. The city of Lille is home to one of his many star-shaped designs.
Vauban was responsible for expanding this city's fortress on the Bock promontory, which was nicknamed the "Gibraltar of the North."

works.name: Versailles and Artistic Patronage
works.description: Louis was the era's dominant art patron, expanding Versailles from a hunting lodge into a palace complex decorated by Charles Le Brun and designed chiefly by the Hardouin-Mansart family, and sitting for the era's defining royal portraits and allegorical ceiling paintings.

clues.clue: Named Charles Le Brun "first painter," who decorated the Hall of Mirrors, the Ambassador Staircase, and the Salons of War and Peace, and whom Louis called the greatest French painter of all time
examples: This ruler named Charles Le Brun as "first painter" and commissioned him to paint the Hall of Mirrors in his royal residence.
According to Louis, the "greatest French painter of all time" was an artist with this surname who decorated the Hall of Mirrors and the Salons of War and Peace.

clues.clue: Le Brun's painting style, influenced by Nicolas Poussin, became known as the namesake "Louis XIV style," which had three periods
examples: Nicolas Poussin influenced the namesake artistic style of this ruler, who employed Charles Le Brun as a court painter.
Name this king who had three "periods" in his namesake style.

clues.clue: Commissioned Le Brun's The Family of Darius Before Alexander, in which Sisygambis mistakenly kneels to Hephaestion instead of Alexander; as patron of the Academy, formulated a "hierarchy of genres" placing history painting above still life
examples: This art patron commissioned a painting set at a tent where Sisygambis mistakenly kneels to Hephaestion instead of the title character.
Under this patron of The Family of Darius Before Alexander, a "hierarchy of genres" was formulated with history painting at the top and still lives at the bottom.

clues.clue: Jules Hardouin-Mansart, chief architect, designed the Place Vendome, the Hall of Mirrors, the Grand Trianon, and the Orangerie; his grand-uncle François Mansart (namesake of the mansard roof) had earlier contributed to Louis's classicist style
examples: This chief architect of Louis XIV designed the Place Vendome as well as the Hall of Mirrors and other Versailles buildings such as the Grand Trianon and the Orangerie.
Mansart worked with this ruler's longtime minister Cardinal Mazarin and contributed to this ruler's preferred classicist style.

clues.clue: Andre Le Notre landscaped the Versailles gardens as the palace was expanded
examples: Andre Le Notre landscaped this Sun King's Palace of Versailles expansions.

clues.clue: Rejected a palace facade design by Gian Lorenzo Bernini in favor of a colonnade by Claude Perrault; Bernini's unflattering bust/statue of Louis was later balanced by a bronze equestrian statue by François Girardon (modified into Marcus Curtius) in the Place Vendome
examples: This man rejected a design for a palace facade from Gian Lorenzo Bernini in favor of a colonnade by Claude Perrault.
Name this French king whose ugly statue by Bernini was balanced with a gigantic bronze equestrian statue by Girardon in the Place Vendome.

clues.clue: Sat for Hyacinthe Rigaud's official "swagger" portrait — a full-length depiction in a blue robe embroidered with gold fleurs-de-lis, holding a scepter, with a sword hilt visible
examples: The hilt of a sword can be seen behind this ruler's blue robe embroidered with a gold fleur-de-lis as he holds a scepter in a portrait by Hyacinthe Rigaud.
Rigaud painted a full-length swagger portrait of this French king, who also ordered the construction of Versailles.

clues.clue: In Jean-Antoine Watteau's The Shop-Sign of Gersaint, a worker boxes away a portrait of Louis as a woman in pink looks on; Louis also brought the Gobelins tapestry factory to prominence
examples: At an art boutique, a woman in a pink dress watches a man box up a portrait of this ruler in Antoine Watteau's The Shop Sign of Gersaint.
This royal, who brought the Gobelins tapestry factory into prominence, appears in a portrait being boxed away in Watteau's Shop-Sign at Gersaint.

clues.clue: Appears in allegorical Versailles ceiling paintings: standing opposite Glory (seated on a cloud above putti playing cards), carrying a golden tiller while gazing at his reflection in Minerva's shield, and standing on a floral golden carpet beneath a gold-engraved figure of Justice
examples: In a gigantic ceiling painting, this figure stands opposite Glory, who sits on a cloud, and above two putti playing cards.
In a ceiling painting, this man carries a massive golden tiller and gazes at his reflection in Minerva's shield.

works.name: Musical and Theatrical Patronage
works.description: Louis, an accomplished ballet dancer who famously portrayed Apollo, made his court the center of French Baroque music and theater, chiefly through his decades-long relationship with composer Jean-Baptiste Lully.

clues.clue: Danced the role of Apollo in the Ballet Royal de la Nuit, a self-presentation that gave him the nickname the "Sun King"
examples: This ballet-dancing monarch liked to portray Roman Emperors, and he received his nickname the "Sun King" for portraying Apollo.
An accomplished ballet dancer, this monarch appeared in the Ballet Royal de la Nuit as the god Apollo, part of their self-presentation as the "Sun King".

clues.clue: Employed Jean-Baptiste Lully as royal music master/court composer for most of his life; Lully pioneered tragédie en musique (beginning with Cadmus et Hermione) and the French overture, and wrote incidental music for Molière plays like Le Bourgeois Gentilhomme and The Pleasures of the Enchanted Isle
examples: name this king who employed Francois Couperin and Jean-Baptiste Lully at Versailles.
Lully was appointed royal music master by this monarch.

clues.clue: Lully's opera Atys was known as "the king's opera" for seemingly depicting Louis's real-life love triangle with the queen and Madame de Maintenon
examples: Lully's opera Atys was known as "the king's opera" for seemingly depicting this "sun" king's real-life love triangle with the queen and Madame de Maintenon.

clues.clue: An extravagant revival of a Lully opera (95-voice choir, 80 dancers) inaugurated an opera hall that Louis left unfinished; while premiering his Te Deum, Lully (Louis's Master of Music) contracted gangrene after stabbing his own foot with a conducting staff
examples: In an extravagant revival of an opera dedicated to this person, a 95-voice choir and 80 dancers helped inaugurate an opera hall that this person left unfinished.
While premiering his Te Deum, this ruler's Master of Music contracted gangrene after stabbing himself with a conducting staff.

clues.clue: Composer François Couperin ("le Grand") consecrated Louis's chapel organ and was named court harpsichordist after Louis's death; his motets, along with Lully's, were commissioned by Louis
examples: After he consecrated this ruler's chapel organ, a composer nicknamed "Le Grand" was named court harpsichordist following this ruler's death.
Many of Lully and Couperin's motets were commissioned by this French monarch, who employed Lully for most of his life.

clues.clue: Composer Marc-Antoine Charpentier, protected by the Duchess of Guise, flouted Lully's monopoly by writing music for Molière's The Imaginary Invalid
examples: Protected by the Duchess of Guise, this composer flouted Lully's monopoly, writing music for Moliere's The Imaginary Invalid.

clues.clue: Befriended Lully while dancing together in the Ballet Royal de la Nuit; Lully later wrote a grand motet ironically celebrating Louis's recovery from surgery
examples: One composer befriended this person while dancing in the Royal Ballet of the Night and later wrote a grand motet that ironically celebrated this person's recovery from surgery.

works.name: Mistresses, Marriage, and the Affair of the Poisons
works.description: Louis's romantic life shaped his court politics: his marriage to Maria Theresa of Spain came only after his first love was banished, mistresses Louise de La Valliere and Madame de Montespan bore him children, Montespan was implicated in the sensational Affair of the Poisons, and Louis secretly remarried Madame de Maintenon.

clues.clue: Mazarin's niece Anna Maria Mancini was Louis's first love but was banished from court so he could marry Maria Theresa of Spain instead
examples: Mazarin's niece Anna Maria Mancini was the first love of this French monarch, and as a result was banished from court so this "Sun King" could marry Maria Theresa of Spain instead.

clues.clue: Had an early love affair with Françoise Scarron (later Madame de Maintenon), whose brother-in-law Philippe I, Duke of Orléans won the Battle of Cassel
examples: Identify this king who had an early love affair with Francoise Scarron. His pretty-much openly gay brother Philippe I, Duke of Orleans, won the Battle of Cassel.

clues.clue: Madame de Montespan, his mistress, was implicated in the Affair of the Poisons, in which fortune-teller La Voisin confessed to selling illicit poisons and was said to have murdered thousands of infants; a tribunal under Nicolas de la Reynie sentenced 36 people to death
examples: Name this 17th century French king whose mistress, the Madame de Montespan, was implicated in the Affair of the Poisons.
The most notable aristocrat implicated was this French king's mistress, the Madame de Montespan.

clues.clue: Mazarin's other niece, Olympia Mancini, became an influential mistress before being expelled during the Affair of the Poisons for plotting with La Voisin
examples: Mazarin's niece Olympia Mancini married into the House of Savoy, but became an influential mistress of Louis XIV until she was expelled during this scandal for plotting with the fortune teller La Voisin.

clues.clue: Secretly married Madame de Maintenon as his second wife; she founded the Maison Royale de Saint-Louis (Saint-Cyr) to educate noble girls
examples: Madame de Maintenon, the secret second wife of this ruler, founded the Maison Royale de Saint-Louis to offer educational opportunities to noble girls.

works.name: Illegitimate Children
works.description: Louis fathered a number of illegitimate children with his mistresses Louise de La Valliere and Madame de Montespan, whom he legitimized and married into the royal family, provoking recurring controversy at court.

clues.clue: Louis Alexandre, Count of Toulouse and son of Madame de Montespan, became Grand Admiral of France at age 5
examples: Louis Alexandre, the Count of Toulouse, and Grand Admiral of France from the age of 5 was one of these people.

clues.clue: Marie Anne, Princesse de Conti, was a daughter of Louise de La Valliere
examples: Marie Anne, the Princess of Conti, and daughter of Louise de La Valliere was one of these people.

clues.clue: The Duke of Maine (son of Montespan) married Louise Bénédicte; his correspondence with Giulio Alberoni was part of the Cellamare conspiracy to replace the Regency
examples: One of these people married Louise Benedicte and, as Duke of Maine, his correspondence with Giulio Alberoni was part of a conspiracy to replace the Regency.

clues.clue: The Duke of Maine's sister (also illegitimate) married her first cousin Philippe of Orléans and became wife of the Regent for the young Louis XV
examples: In addition to that participant in the Cellamare conspiracy and son of Athenais de Montespan, his sister, who was also one of these people, was married to her first cousin Philippe of Orleans.

clues.clue: Marriages of Louis's illegitimate children to Princes of the Blood caused controversy at court
examples: The marriage of these people to Princes of the Blood caused controversy in the court of the father of these people, who instigated the War of the Devolution.

comprehensive_summary: Louis XIV (1638-1715) was king of France for 72 years, the longest reign of any monarch in history, and is remembered as the "Sun King" (le Roi Soleil) for the role of Apollo he danced in the Ballet Royal de la Nuit, and for the (possibly apocryphal) declaration "I am the state" (L'état, c'est moi). During his minority, Cardinal Jules Mazarin — successor to the "Red Eminence" as chief minister — tutored Louis and signed the Peace of Westphalia while suppressing the Fronde, a civil war named for the slingshots used by rioting Parisian crowds, which unfolded in phases led by the parlements and then the princes of the blood, including the Grand Condé's revolt, at the Battle of the Faubourg St. Antoine. Once in power, Louis relied on ministers Jean-Baptiste Colbert, whose mercantilist economic policy funded the Canal du Midi, and Nicolas Fouquet, whose earlier extravagance at Vaux-le-Vicomte doomed him to imprisonment at Pignerol, as well as military engineer Vauban, who designed and besieged fortresses like the Citadel of Lille and Namur. Louis fought a chain of wars: the War of Devolution (over the unpaid dowry of his wife Maria Theresa of Spain), the Franco-Dutch War (ended by the Treaties of Nijmegen), the War of the League of Augsburg against a coalition led by William III, and the War of the Spanish Succession, which installed his grandson Philip of Anjou as Philip V of Spain and ended at Utrecht with the Bourbon dynasty split and forced to grant Britain the asiento. A devout Catholic, Louis revoked the Edict of Nantes via the 1685 Edict of Fontainebleau, driving out the Huguenots, razed the Jansenist stronghold of Port-Royal Abbey, persecuted the Quietism promoted by Jeanne Guyon and his secret wife Madame de Maintenon, and asserted Gallican independence from Rome via the 1682 Declaration of the Clergy of France, feuding chiefly with Pope Innocent XI. He also issued the Code Noir regulating slavery in the French Empire. Louis transformed the Palace of Versailles from a hunting lodge into Europe's grandest residence, employing Charles Le Brun as "first painter" to decorate the Hall of Mirrors, architect Jules Hardouin-Mansart to design the Place Vendome and Grand Trianon, and Andre Le Notre to landscape the gardens; he sat for Hyacinthe Rigaud's iconic "swagger" portrait and appears boxed away in a portrait within Jean-Antoine Watteau's The Shop-Sign of Gersaint. His court composer Jean-Baptiste Lully pioneered tragédie en musique and the French overture, while François Couperin and Marc-Antoine Charpentier also composed for the court. Louis's romantic life was equally consequential: his first love Anna Maria Mancini was banished so he could marry Maria Theresa, his mistress Madame de Montespan was implicated in the Affair of the Poisons alongside fortune-teller La Voisin, and he later secretly married Madame de Maintenon. His illegitimate children by La Valliere and Montespan, including the Count of Toulouse and the Duke of Maine, were legitimized and married into the royal family, sparking recurring controversy at court.
recursive_suggestions: Jean-Baptiste Lully — recurring near-standalone answerline (3x recurring bonus part), tightly linked to Louis's court music
Affair of the Poisons — recurring separate answerline (2x), distinct scandal topic
Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban — recurring military-engineer answerline (2x as own bonus part)
Nicolas Fouquet — distinct finance-minister figure with his own downfall narrative
Illegitimate Children of Louis XIV — has independent tossup presence (2012 Chicago Open Doubles); could become standalone if it recurs further
Madame de Montespan / Madame de Maintenon — recurring mistress figures that could eventually merit their own pages

Mime performance — a 2020 ACF Regionals tossup notes Louis XIV exiled the Italian Players (for insulting his mistress), giving rise to French pantomime, but the tossup's actual answer is "mime performance," not Louis XIV; excluded from this analysis

Louis XIII — a 2016 ARTSEE tossup on the ambiguous answerline "Louis" (accepting Louis XIII or XIV) actually clues Peter Paul Rubens's Marie de Medici cycle, depicting the young Louis XIII's coming-of-age and reconciliation with his mother; excluded here as it describes Louis XIII, not Louis XIV
